message.content:
PortSwigger’s Web Security Academy currently groups its material into **server-side**, **client-side**, and **advanced** topic areas, and the canonical topic index is the Academy “All topics” page[7]. Below is the full topic list matching the categories you requested, with the relevant vulnerability/check, related CWE where applicable, and the canonical PortSwigger topic URL pattern under `portswigger.net/web-security/<slug>`[3][7].

| Topic | What it covers | Related CWE | Canonical URL |
|---|---|---|---|
| SQL injection | Injection of attacker-controlled SQL into database queries; includes discovering, exploiting, and blind/UNION-based SQLi[1][2][3] | CWE-89 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/sql-injection` |
| Authentication | Weaknesses in login, MFA, and other authentication mechanisms[1][2][7] | CWE-287 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/authentication` |
| Path traversal | Traversal of filesystem paths to access unintended files[1][2][7] | CWE-22 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/path-traversal` |
| Command injection | Injection of OS commands into server-side command execution; PortSwigger labels this topic “OS command injection” in some places[1][2][3][7] | CWE-77 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/command-injection` |
| Business logic vulnerabilities | Flaws in application workflows or assumptions that let attackers bypass intended behavior[1][2][7] | CWE-840 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/business-logic-vulnerabilities` |
| Information disclosure | Exposure of sensitive data through errors, debug output, metadata, or misconfiguration[1][2][7] | CWE-200 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/information-disclosure` |
| Access control | Broken authorization, IDOR-style issues, privilege escalation, and access control bypasses[1][2][7] | CWE-284 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/access-control` |
| File upload vulnerabilities | Unsafe upload handling leading to code execution, content smuggling, or other abuse[2][3] | CWE-434 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/file-upload` |
| SSRF | Server-side requests to attacker-chosen destinations, including blind SSRF[1][2][3][7] | CWE-918 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/ssrf` |
| XXE | XML external entity injection, including blind XXE exploitation[1][2][3][7] | CWE-611 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/xxe` |
| NoSQL injection | Injection into NoSQL query languages or document filters[2][3] | CWE-943 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/nosql-injection` |
| API testing | Testing API endpoints for authentication, authorization, input handling, and business-logic flaws[2][3] | CWE-306 / CWE-862 / CWE-89, context-dependent | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/api-testing` |
| Web cache poisoning | Manipulating cache keys or downstream responses so a cache serves attacker-influenced content[1][2][3] | CWE-525 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/web-cache-poisoning` |
| HTTP request smuggling | Desynchronizing front-end and back-end request parsing; includes advanced request-smuggling variants[1][2][3] | CWE-444 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/http-request-smuggling` |
| OS command injection | Same topic area as command injection: injection into shell/OS command execution[1][2][3][7] | CWE-77 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/os-command-injection` |
| Server-side template injection | Injection into server-side template engines leading to code execution or data access[1][2][3] | CWE-1336 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/server-side-template-injection` |
| Cross-site scripting (XSS) | Injecting script into web pages; includes reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS coverage[1][2][3] | CWE-79 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/cross-site-scripting` |
| CSRF | Forging authenticated cross-site requests to perform unwanted actions[1][2][3][7] | CWE-352 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/csrf` |
| CORS | Misconfigured cross-origin resource sharing and same-origin policy bypass scenarios[1][2][3] | CWE-942 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/cors` |
| Clickjacking | UI redressing attacks using framed or overlaid content[1][2][3] | CWE-1021 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/clickjacking` |
| DOM-based vulnerabilities | Client-side vulnerabilities in JavaScript and DOM manipulation, including DOM XSS and related sinks/sources[1][3] | CWE-79 / CWE-74, context-dependent | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/dom-based` |
| WebSockets | Security testing for WebSocket message handling, origin checks, and hijacking issues[1][2][3] | CWE-346 / CWE-639, context-dependent | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/websockets` |
| Web cache deception | Forcing dynamic content to be cached as if it were static, exposing user-specific data[2][3] | CWE-525 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/web-cache-deception` |
| Prototype pollution | Polluting JavaScript prototypes to alter application behavior, including client-side and server-side variants[1][2][3] | CWE-1321 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/prototype-pollution` |
| Insecure deserialization | Exploiting unsafe object deserialization to gain code execution or other impact[1][2][3] | CWE-502 | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/insecure-deserialization` |
| GraphQL API | Attacks specific to GraphQL schemas, queries, introspection, and resolver behavior[1][2][3] | CWE-918 / CWE-639 / CWE-200, context-dependent | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/graphql-api` |
| JWT attacks | Weaknesses in JSON Web Token handling, validation, signing, and algorithm selection[1][2][3] | CWE-347 / CWE-345, context-dependent | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/jwt` |
| OAuth | OAuth 2.0 authentication and authorization flaws, including grant-flow and redirect issues[1][2][3] | CWE-601 / CWE-287, context-dependent | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/oauth` |
| Web LLM attacks | Attacks against web applications that integrate large language models, including prompt injection and data leakage scenarios[2][3] | CWE-20 / CWE-74 / CWE-200, context-dependent | `https://portswigger.net/web-security/web-llm-attacks` |

PortSwigger’s own topic index confirms the presence of these topic areas on the Academy landing page and the “All topics” page, while the “All learning materials” page shows the Academy’s broader module structure and some subtopics under each area[1][3][7].
